title: Fix: add ALTER TABLE migration for recipes.slug + drink_recipes.slug
description:
## Why
Production deploy on dietpi is broken: \"no such column: recipes.slug\" / \"no such column: drink_recipes.slug\" from every recipe-listing query.

## Root cause
Commit 9d46bed (\"refactor: simplify slugs to pre-release form\") added the \`slug\` column to \`recipes\` and \`drink_recipes\` by editing the existing \`m20260118_000002_create_recipes\` and \`m20260213_000010_create_drink_recipes\` migrations IN-PLACE, rather than adding a new ALTER TABLE migration. SeaORM tracks already-run migrations by name, so existing DBs (like dietpi's) skip the modified migrations and never gain the column. New code queries the missing column and 500s.

## What's needed
New migration \`m20260424_000012_add_recipe_slugs\`:
1. \`ALTER TABLE recipes ADD COLUMN slug TEXT\` (nullable initially)
2. \`ALTER TABLE drink_recipes ADD COLUMN slug TEXT\` (nullable initially)
3. Backfill: iterate existing rows, compute \`slugify(name)\`, dedupe collisions by appending \`-2\`, \`-3\`, etc., write back
4. Create unique indexes \`idx_recipes_slug\` and \`idx_drink_recipes_slug\`
5. Skip the NOT NULL upgrade — SQLite would require the create-copy-drop-rename dance, and the app always populates slug. Existing fresh DBs already have NOT NULL; this drift is acceptable.

## Verification
- \`cargo test\` green
- Migration applies cleanly on a snapshot of the pre-slug schema
- Migration is no-op on a fresh DB (column already exists from the edited create migration — guard with a column-exists check, or use \`if not exists\` on the indexes)
- Deploy to dietpi via \`just deploy dietpi@dietpi.local\`, verify Recipes tab loads
close_reason: Implemented as new migration m20260424_000012_backfill_recipe_slugs. Idempotent across legacy + fresh DBs via has_column gate. Tests cover backfill, collision dedup, fallback, idempotency, uniqueness. Pushed in 38c01aa. Awaiting deploy verification on dietpi.

title: Bump vite past path-traversal advisory (5.x → 6.4.2+ or 7.x)
description:
## Why
Open dependabot advisory: \"Vite Vulnerable to Path Traversal in Optimized Deps \`.map\` Handling\" (medium). Vulnerable range is `<= 6.4.1`, so our current vite 5.4.21 is affected. `bun update` cannot move us — package.json pins vite to `^5.0.8` and the fix only exists on later majors.

## What's needed
Major bump with coordinated peer bumps:
- `vite` 5.4.21 → 7.x (latest stable; 6.x is patched at 6.4.2+ but already EOL trajectory)
- `@vitejs/plugin-react` 4.x → 5.x (peer-locked to vite major)
- `vitest` 1.6.x → 3.x (peer-locked to vite major)
- `@vitest/ui` matching vitest major
- Verify `vite.config.ts` and `vitest.config.ts` still compile
- Verify dev server (`bun run dev:full`) still proxies `/api` correctly
- Verify production build + `just deploy` (arm64 build) still works

## Risks
- vite 7 dropped Node 18 support; CI + local dev must be on Node 20+
- vitest 3 has reporter / API changes — some test setup may need tweaks
- jsdom 28 → 29 may be pulled along by vitest peer constraints

## Scope decision
Land as one PR: vite + plugin-react + vitest + ui must move together. Splitting adds churn without isolating risk.

## Verification
- `bun run build` clean
- `bun run test` green (currently 295 pass)
- `bun run lint` clean
- Manual smoke test of dev server + a deploy via `just deploy dietpi@dietpi.local`
close_reason: Closed

title: Remove hardcoded family PII from seed_data.rs
description:
server/src/services/seed_data.rs hardcodes the project owner's family — four first names (Steve, Amanda, Vivienne, Cleo) plus birthdates — into the default seed that runs on first DB init. This was tolerable while the repo was effectively private, but it's now visible in the public repo (and was flagged tangentially by Copilot during PR #9 review of the unrelated bd memory).

The seed is called from db.rs line 17 (seed_if_empty) only when the People table is empty, so changing the values won't disturb existing installs.

Options:
1. Replace the hardcoded list with placeholder/example names ('Alex', 'Jordan', 'Sam', 'Pat') and generic birthdates (e.g. all 1990-01-01). Lowest friction; new installs get a working empty-but-not-empty starter.
2. Drop seed_if_empty entirely so a fresh install presents a real empty state, and rely on the family-add UI to onboard. Cleaner long-term; requires confirming the rest of the app handles a People table with zero rows gracefully (MealPlanner, suggestion flows, etc.).
3. Keep seed for local dev only — gate seed_if_empty behind a SEED_DEMO_DATA env var or debug build cfg.

My recommendation: option 1 for the immediate PII removal, then option 2 as a separate cleanup if/when the empty-state UX is ready.

Acceptance criteria:
- No real names or birthdates in version-controlled seed code.
- Existing installations are unaffected (seed only runs when People is empty).
- A fresh install still produces a usable app (either via placeholder seed or via verified empty-state handling).

Files to touch:
- server/src/services/seed_data.rs (the data)
- Possibly server/src/db.rs (if option 2 or 3)
close_reason: Replaced real family names (Steve/Amanda/Vivienne/Cleo) and birthdates in server/src/services/seed_data.rs with placeholders (Alex/Jordan/Sam/Pat) preserving a 2-adult + 2-child age spread. Updated matching assertion in server/tests/service_tests.rs and the example JSON in REQUIREMENTS.md. Follow-up ticket fewd-esj tracks scrubbing the same names from git history.

title: Consolidate workspace deps and remove stale server/Cargo.lock
description:
## Why
Two issues caused duplicate Dependabot PRs (e.g. #11/#12 for rand, #13/#14 for rustls-webpki):

1. **Stale `server/Cargo.lock`** — leftover from before the workspace conversion. Cargo only reads the root `/Cargo.lock` (workspace resolves all members at root), so this file is dead weight Dependabot scans independently.
2. **No `[workspace.dependencies]`** — direct deps are declared independently in each member crate, so updating shared deps requires touching multiple files.

## What's needed
- Delete `/server/Cargo.lock`
- Add `[workspace.package]` for shared metadata (edition, version)
- Add `[workspace.dependencies]` for cross-member shared deps (`tokio`) and ecosystem-pinned families (`sea-orm` + `sea-orm-migration` must stay in lockstep)
- Update `server/Cargo.toml` and `server/migration/Cargo.toml` to inherit via `workspace = true`
- Verify `cargo build --release` and `cargo test` still clean

## Scope
Pre-req for the v2026-04-24.1 hotfix release. Branch + PR (per repo convention for production-touching changes).
notes: Implementation complete on branch chore/workspace-deps-consolidation. Verified: workspace cargo check, cargo test (17 migration tests pass), cargo build --release (no feature-flag drift), cargo fmt/clippy clean. Root Cargo.lock unchanged — pure refactor. Ready for PR.
close_reason: Merged via PR #16 (commit 91028c7). Removed stale server/Cargo.lock and consolidated tokio + sea-orm + sea-orm-migration into [workspace.dependencies]. Future Dependabot PRs should no longer duplicate per-lockfile.

title: Add CI smoke-test for migration drift (run against pre-existing schema snapshot)
description:
## Why
fewd-???-recipe-slug-fix discovered that CI cannot detect when an already-applied migration is edited in place (which silently breaks any DB that ran the original version). CI builds always start from an empty DB, so the modified migration runs from scratch and works. Production DBs that already ran the original don't get the schema change.

## What's needed
Add a CI step (or test) that:
1. Restores a checked-in SQLite snapshot of an older schema state (e.g. one captured at each release tag)
2. Boots the server / runs migrations against it
3. Asserts the schema matches what current code expects (e.g. by running representative queries: list recipes, list drink_recipes, list meals)
4. Fails CI if a query 500s on the migrated old DB

## Implementation sketch
- Add \`server/test-fixtures/schema-snapshots/v2026-04-24.db\` (or similar — capture per release)
- Add an integration test under \`server/tests/migration_compat.rs\` that opens the snapshot, runs migrations, executes representative queries
- Update \`just ci\` and \`.github/workflows/ci.yml\` to run it

## Risks
- Snapshot maintenance: a snapshot per release tag is the cleanest contract but adds a manual step (or could be automated via a release hook)
- Binary diffs in git are noisy — consider committing the snapshot as a SQL dump (\`.sql\` text) and rebuilding the DB from it at test time

## Scope decision
Single PR after the slug-fix lands. Snapshot strategy decision is the design call worth deferring until we look at file sizes.
notes:
Lesson learned during fewd-nwi rollout (2026-04-24):

The migration's first attempt used \`SchemaManager::has_column()\`, which is gated on sea-orm-migration's \`sqlx-sqlite\` feature. The migration crate's runtime deps don't enable that feature (it's only enabled transitively in fewd-server's sea-orm dep, which doesn't propagate to sea-orm-migration). Tests passed because dev-dependencies merged the feature in for test builds, but the release build panicked at runtime: \`thread 'main' panicked: Sqlite feature is off\` at \`sea-orm-migration-1.1.20/src/manager.rs:120\`.

This means the smoke test must be a RELEASE BUILD running against a snapshot DB, not just \`cargo test\`. \`cargo test\`'s feature unification with dev-deps masks runtime feature gaps.

Specifically the smoke test should:
1. Build server in --release
2. Boot it with DATABASE_PATH pointing at a checked-in snapshot
3. Confirm no panic on startup AND that representative queries succeed

Workaround in fewd-nwi: replaced has_column() with raw \`PRAGMA table_info\` query — no feature flag needed.

title: Split IngredientDto.name into name + prep (purchase form vs. preparation form)
description:
Today IngredientDto.name bundles the purchasable thing and the preparation state into one string ('garlic cloves, thinly sliced', 'lemon, cut into wedges for serving'). Two downstream problems:

1. Shopping-list readability: the list surfaces prep state as if it's what you buy. Nobody buys pre-sliced garlic.

2. Aggregation can't collapse across recipes: three recipes with 'garlic, minced', 'garlic cloves, thinly sliced', and 'whole garlic cloves' read as three different ingredients and produce three shopping-list lines instead of one.

Claude (MCP consumer) flagged both when preparing a real grocery list.

Data-model change:

    pub struct IngredientDto {
        pub name: String,           // 'garlic'       — the thing you buy
        pub prep: Option<String>,   // 'thinly sliced' — how the recipe uses it
        pub amount: IngredientAmountDto,
        pub unit: Unit,              // see fewd-lm3
        pub notes: Option<String>,
    }

Recipe instructions/UI compose display as 'name, prep' where prep is present. Shopping list aggregates by name alone.

**Scope**:

1. Add the field everywhere IngredientDto appears: dto.rs, MCP IngredientOut in schemas/common.rs, CreateRecipeInput, recipe-edit UI form, cooking-mode display, URL-import recipe parser, shopping aggregator.

2. **Migration**: per fewd-data-migration-posture memory, don't hedge on existing rows. One-shot migration walks every recipe, runs each ingredient name through either (a) an LLM classifier (Claude API call given the ingredient, returns structured name + prep) or (b) a rule-based splitter keyed on 'name, prep-verb' patterns like 'X, minced' / 'X, thinly sliced' / 'X, cut into Y'. Dry-run the migration, spot-check a handful, then commit. For recipes where the split is ambiguous, leave prep=None and the name unchanged as an escape hatch.

3. Shopping aggregator: group by name (case-insensitive) ignoring prep. Interacts mildly with fewd-wm6 (staples classification) since the classifier keys on name — a split name is cleaner to classify on.

**Edge case to handle during migration**: 'boneless skinless chicken breast' vs 'whole chicken' are the same name but different purchases. Naive 'aggregate by name' is wrong here. Options: (a) treat name strings as already distinguishing ('chicken breast' vs 'whole chicken' are different names, period), (b) add a purchase_form field as a third axis, (c) accept occasional mis-aggregation. Lean (a) — simplest, and the ambiguity surfaces naturally in the LLM / rule splitter.
acceptance_criteria: IngredientDto has split name + Option<String> prep. All consumers (Recipe edit UI, cooking mode, MCP schemas, shopping aggregator, URL parser) handle the new shape. One-shot migration re-splits existing recipes via LLM-assisted or rule-based classifier, with a dry-run + spot-check step before commit. Shopping aggregation collapses prep variants of the same name into one line. Edge case of 'chicken breast' vs 'whole chicken' handled (chose strategy (a): distinct names = distinct purchases).
notes: Preserve these existing-behavior invariants when touching ShoppingService output (called out by live Claude Desktop consumer as the tool's strongest properties): (1) per-ingredient source breakdown — each aggregated item carries a list of contributing (meal_id, date, meal_type, recipe_name, amount) so the consumer can answer 'why is there extra flour?'; (2) aggregation shape is total + sources, not total OR sources — keep both; (3) range math preserves Range { min, max } through scaling, doesn't collapse to Single on convenient simplification.

title: Shopping list: classify + separate pantry staples (don't filter, split)
description:
Pantry staples — kosher salt, black pepper, olive oil, garlic powder, smoked paprika, red pepper flakes, etc. — appear in the shopping list today as line items indistinguishable from real shopping needs. They're noise unless the user is actually out of them, and the server can't know that.

Right shape, based on live Claude feedback: DON'T filter them out. Return them in a separate section so consumers (LLM or UI) can present 'stuff to buy' and 'stuff to double-check' distinctly. A shopping list that says 'buy these 8 items; also verify the smoked paprika' is more useful than one that silently drops the staple — especially for an LLM that can phrase the verification naturally in conversation.

Response shape:

    {
      "items_to_buy": [...],
      "pantry_staples_to_verify": [...]
    }

Classification approach: COMPUTED at list-generation time, not stored per-ingredient. A self-contained classifier module taking (ingredient_name, unit, total_amount) → bool. Rules:

1. Small-measurement units (tsp, tbsp, pinch, dash) → staple. These units almost universally indicate a spice or seasoning that you buy in a jar and use sparingly.

2. Name-based allowlist for units where the unit alone isn't diagnostic: salt, pepper, kosher salt, black pepper, olive oil, avocado oil, vegetable oil, butter (small amounts), flour, sugar, baking powder, baking soda, vinegar (small amounts), common spices (cumin, paprika, garlic powder, onion powder, chili powder, oregano, thyme, basil, bay leaves, etc). Document the list; expand as false negatives surface.

Why not stored per-ingredient: would require every recipe author (human or LLM) to classify, plus UI affordances, plus a migration. The accuracy gain over heuristics is modest for a household app. If false positives become a problem ('this recipe genuinely calls for salt as a key ingredient'), add an optional per-ingredient override later — the heuristic stays the default.

Connects to fewd-kif, which exposes the two-section response through MCP and UI.
acceptance_criteria: ShoppingService returns a structured response with items_to_buy and pantry_staples_to_verify as separate arrays. The staple classifier is a standalone function with clear rules, covered by unit tests for each category (small-measurement units, allowlisted names, legitimate non-staples that must NOT be classified as staple). Allowlist documented in the source. Works on both the raw aggregated amounts and the rounded amounts from fewd-p3j.
notes: Preserve these existing-behavior invariants when touching ShoppingService output (called out by live Claude Desktop consumer as the tool's strongest properties): (1) per-ingredient source breakdown — each aggregated item carries a list of contributing (meal_id, date, meal_type, recipe_name, amount) so the consumer can answer 'why is there extra flour?'; (2) aggregation shape is total + sources, not total OR sources — keep both; (3) range math preserves Range { min, max } through scaling, doesn't collapse to Single on convenient simplification.

title: Shopping list: round up to grocery-realistic amounts (counts + weights + volumes)
description:
The current shopping list returns mathematically exact scaled amounts — 1.75 lb potatoes, 0.88 lemons, 0.875 pinch red pepper flakes. Nobody shops in those quantities. Introduce a 'shopping amounts' view that rounds up each aggregated ingredient to something you'd actually buy.

Rules in order of trivial-to-heuristic:

1. Count-unit ingredients (each, whole, head, clove, bunch): ceil to the next whole integer. 0.88 lemons → 1, 2.4 heads garlic → 3.

2. Weight units (lb, oz, g, kg) and volume units with grocery equivalents (cup, quart, gallon, liter): round up using a sliding scale. Small amounts (< 0.5 unit) → nearest 0.25; medium (< 2 units) → nearest 0.5; large (≥ 2 units) → nearest 1. So 1.75 lb → 2 lb, 0.3 cup → 0.5 cup, 4.2 lb → 5 lb.

3. Small-measurement units (tsp, tbsp, pinch, dash): tracked separately (see the pantry-staples bead) since these are almost always things you already own.

Implementation: new service-layer function, e.g. ShoppingService::get_shopping_list_rounded(start, end) or ShoppingService::get_shopping_list(start, end, format: ShoppingFormat) where ShoppingFormat has variants Recipe (current behavior) and Shopping (the rounded version). Returns a GroceryListItem DTO with display_amount: String (e.g. '2 lb'), display_unit: String, raw_amount: Option<IngredientAmountDto> for secondary display, and whatever_round_occurred flags for UI hints.

Unit-conversion logic belongs in the existing unit_converter module. Rounding tables and category classification (count vs weight vs small-measurement) can live alongside.

Integration is a follow-up bead.
acceptance_criteria: New function in ShoppingService returns rounded GroceryListItem values. Count units always ceil to integers. Weight/volume units follow the sliding rounding scale. Small-measurement units passed through as-is (filter work happens in the pantry bead). Unit tests cover each ingredient-category rule with edge values (exact round numbers, just-below thresholds, very small, very large).
notes: Preserve these existing-behavior invariants when touching ShoppingService output (called out by live Claude Desktop consumer as the tool's strongest properties): (1) per-ingredient source breakdown — each aggregated item carries a list of contributing (meal_id, date, meal_type, recipe_name, amount) so the consumer can answer 'why is there extra flour?'; (2) aggregation shape is total + sources, not total OR sources — keep both; (3) range math preserves Range { min, max } through scaling, doesn't collapse to Single on convenient simplification.

title: Recipe discovery redesign: list_curated_recipes + search_recipes with persona filters
description:
Two concerns at 150+ recipe scale: unfiltered dumps are a context tax (~8-15k tokens per call), and there's no direct way for the LLM to filter against family members' dislikes without pulling every recipe's ingredients and cross-checking in-conversation.

Design:

1. RENAME list_recipes → list_curated_recipes. No params. Returns a bounded ~30-item shortlist (favorites + recently-made + top-rated, de-duped). Name makes clear this is a selection, not an enumeration.

2. EXPAND search_recipes. Required: at least one non-trivial filter (empty / query='*' both rejected with pointer to list_curated_recipes). Filters:
   - query (current substring on name)
   - tags: Vec<String>
   - max_total_time_minutes: i32
   - min_rating: f64
   - is_favorite: bool
   - unmade_since_days: i32
   - excludes_for_persons: Vec<String> — reject any recipe whose ingredient NAMES contain any of the listed active persons' dislikes (simple case-insensitive substring; explicitly document that 'olive oil' is rejected when 'olive' is disliked so the LLM can plan around false positives)

3. NOT in this pass: favorites-based matching (ranking problem, not filter), dietary-goals matching (free-form text, needs LLM judgment), pagination (shortlist is bounded, search is filter-bounded).

Full-archive dump intentionally not exposed — not an LLM use case; web UI is there for humans who want to scroll everything.

Implementation: push all filters into the SeaORM query, not post-filtering in Rust, so it scales with catalog size.
acceptance_criteria: list_curated_recipes returns ≤30 blended favorites/recent/top-rated with documented policy. search_recipes rejects bare calls with a helpful error. excludes_for_persons resolves names via the existing MealLookups pattern, loads each person's dislikes, and filters at the DB layer (LIKE / LOWER matches on ingredient names). Docs call out the substring-match caveats. No tool returns the full archive.

title: Scrub pre-d92 family PII from git history
description:
After fewd-d92 removes hardcoded family names from HEAD, the real names (Steve/Amanda/Vivienne/Cleo + real birthdates) still exist in every historical commit that touched server/src/services/seed_data.rs — back through the initial commit. The Person-entity example in REQUIREMENTS.md has the same issue. Scrub these from history via git filter-repo, then force-push main.

Pre-conditions confirmed by owner (2026-04-21):
- Repo has been public only a few days.
- No known external clones or collaborators.
- Accepts that GitHub's ~90-day cached commit views will expire naturally; no GitHub Support ticket needed.
- Accepts that the fork at turboladen/fewd (source of PR #9) will have divergent history; coordinate ad-hoc if needed.

Approach sketch:
- Install git filter-repo (Homebrew).
- Use --replace-text with a substitutions file covering the four real first names and the four real birthdates, mapping each to its fewd-d92 placeholder equivalent so old commits become compilable.
- Verify locally: fresh clone of the rewritten repo, cargo build across a sample of historical SHAs.
- Force-push main. Do NOT touch refs/dolt/data (beads state is independent).
- Post-push: delete local .git/refs/original/, re-clone to confirm, update any bd memory entries that reference pre-rewrite SHAs.
acceptance_criteria: git log -S'Steve' -- server/ returns no results across full history. Same for Amanda, Vivienne, Cleo. REQUIREMENTS.md in every commit no longer contains those names. Build succeeds at HEAD after rewrite; spot-check 2-3 historical SHAs.

title: Cooking mode: ADHD-friendly enhancements (step check-off, current-step highlight, ingredient check-off)
description:
Builds on fewd-pqb (the calm focused view). After shipping the base cooking mode and using it in the kitchen a few times, add interaction features that help cooks (especially those with ADHD) stay anchored to where they are in the recipe.

Scope:
- Tap a step card to mark it complete (fades to ~50% opacity, number gets a soft strikethrough or subtle dim)
- Tap an ingredient to mark it as added (line-through style)
- Visual highlight on the 'current' step (the topmost not-yet-completed step) — gentle terracotta border or background tint
- Optional: persist progress per-recipe in localStorage so reloading or briefly leaving doesn't wipe place
- All state local — no server persistence

Why higher priority than multi-recipe selection:
- Daily impact for the actual kitchen user
- The base CookingView component should be designed to absorb these features; doing them next while the design context is fresh keeps the architecture clean

Acceptance:
- Tapping a step toggles its completed state visually
- Tapping an ingredient toggles its 'added' state visually
- Current step (first incomplete) is visually distinct
- Reloading mid-cook restores progress (if persistence is included)
- Exiting cooking mode and re-entering: TBD — discuss whether progress should reset (likely yes for first-cut)
- Existing cooking-mode behavior (chrome hidden, wake lock, escape/exit) is preserved

title: Cover ErrorBoundary with tests
description: ErrorBoundary prevents one bad component from white-screening the entire app. Currently untested — a regression would only surface when a real production error fires. Small scope, high blast radius if broken.
acceptance_criteria:
- src/components/ErrorBoundary.test.tsx created
- Tests: (1) renders children when no error; (2) renders fallback UI when child throws; (3) console.error suppressed/mocked in test; (4) reset behavior if the component exposes one
close_reason: Added ErrorBoundary.test.tsx with 4 tests: children render, fallback UI on throw, console.error logging contract, reset behavior. All 78 frontend tests pass. Lint + dprint clean.

title: Test hook infrastructure + usePeople exemplar
description: Frontend hook coverage is 0% (0/10 hooks tested). A broken TanStack Query mutation invalidation silently desyncs the UI from the backend — something component tests don't catch. This issue establishes the hook-testing pattern and implements usePeople as the reference that unblocks the other 9 hooks and the integration tests.
acceptance_criteria:
- Fetch-mocking pattern picked (MSW preferred, or a light vi.spyOn(global, 'fetch') shim) and extracted into a test utility under src/test/
- QueryClient wrapper helper available for renderHook calls
- src/hooks/usePeople.test.ts covers: list (success/error/loading), create+invalidate, update, delete
- All tests pass via 'bun run test'
- Pattern is copy-pasteable by the follow-up hook tests and integration tests
close_reason: Established hook-testing pattern: src/test/queryClient.tsx (createTestQueryClient + createQueryWrapper with retry:false), src/test/fetchMock.ts (vi.spyOn fetch with exact URL match), src/hooks/usePeople.test.ts (10 tests: list success/error/loading, usePerson enabled flag, create+POST, create invalidation behavioral+contract, update, delete with 204). All 88 tests pass. Pattern is copy-pasteable for fewd-hye and the 4 integration-test issues.

title: Add distinct shareable URLs for recipes
description: Recipes (food and drink) currently aren't individually addressable via URL, so they can't be shared with others by link. Each recipe should have its own route (e.g., /recipes/:id, /cocktails/recipes/:id) that deep-links to a detail view. This enables sharing links to specific recipes and improves navigation UX (browser back/forward, bookmarks). Requires introducing a router (e.g., react-router) since the app currently uses tab-based state in App.tsx.
close_reason: Added react-router-dom. Routes: /family, /meals/{planner,templates,shopping}, /recipes, /recipes/:id, /cocktails/{suggest,bar}, /cocktails/recipes, /cocktails/recipes/:id. Detail pages use existing useRecipe(id)/useDrinkRecipe(id) hooks. Server SPA fallback already handles deep-link refresh. Verified via playwright smoke test.

title: Promote Recipes to top-level nav tab
description: Split Recipes out of Meals sub-tabs into its own top-level tab. Navigation changes from Family|Meals|Cocktails to Family|Meals|Recipes|Cocktails. Meals sub-tabs become Planner|Templates|Shopping. RecipeManager is already self-contained (no props, independent hooks/routes), so this is purely a navigation restructure in App.tsx plus doc updates to CLAUDE.md/REQUIREMENTS.md/IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md.
close_reason: Promoted Recipes to top-level nav tab in App.tsx and updated CLAUDE.md docs

title: Evaluate Tailwind 3 → 4 migration
description:
## Why
Tailwind 4 is a near-rewrite: new Oxide/Lightning CSS engine, CSS-first config (\`@theme\`), removal of \`tailwind.config.js\`-based design tokens in favor of CSS variables, and a vite-first build path. Worth evaluating because we have a substantial design-system layer in \`src/index.css\` (\`@layer components\` tokens, \`@layer utilities\` animations) and a custom palette in \`tailwind.config.js\`.

## What's needed
- `tailwindcss` 3.4.19 → 4.x
- Migrate `tailwind.config.js` colors (primary/secondary/accent/surface) to `@theme` CSS variables in `src/index.css`
- Migrate font config (Playfair Display, DM Sans) to `@theme`
- Verify `@layer components` tokens (`.btn`, `.input`, `.card`, `.tag`, panel variants) still resolve
- Verify `@layer utilities` animations (animate-fade-in, animate-expand etc.) still emit
- Use the official upgrade tool (`npx @tailwindcss/upgrade@latest`) as a starting point, then audit diff manually
- Possibly switch to `@tailwindcss/vite` plugin for faster builds

## Risks
- Custom utilities and component classes need manual review — upgrade tool catches most but not all
- Browser baseline shifts to Safari 16.4+ / Chrome 111+ (we currently target safari13/chrome100 in vite.config.ts — consider lowering scope or accepting the bump)
- Visual regressions on the design system are likely; needs a manual UI sweep across all top-level routes

## Scope decision
Evaluate first (read upgrade-tool output + check breakage list against our `index.css`), then decide whether to do it now or defer. Do not auto-bump.

## Verification
- `bun run build` clean
- Visual diff across Family / Meals / Recipes / Cocktails routes vs. baseline
- Animation utilities still work (recipe expand, toast slide-in, etc.)

title: Bump React 18 → 19
description:
## Why
React 19 is GA. Brings the new compiler, Actions, useActionState, simplified ref API, and improved Suspense. Not security-driven — this is feature/maintenance hygiene.

## What's needed
- `react` 18.3.1 → 19.x
- `react-dom` 18.3.1 → 19.x
- `@types/react` 18.3.28 → 19.x
- `@types/react-dom` 18.3.7 → 19.x
- Verify peer compat with `@tanstack/react-query` (already on 5.x — supports 19)
- Verify peer compat with `react-router-dom` 7.x (supports 19)
- Verify peer compat with `@testing-library/react` 16.x (already 19-ready)
- Verify peer compat with `@vitejs/plugin-react` (current 4.x supports 19; coupled to fewd-5tc if vite is bumped)

## Risks
- The string-ref `ReactElement.ref` migration may surface in components that pass refs
- Default `propTypes` and `defaultProps` for function components are gone (we use TS — not affected)
- StrictMode double-invokes `useEffect` cleanups in 19 (already true in 18 dev mode)

## Scope decision
Standalone PR. Coordinate landing order with fewd-5tc only if @vitejs/plugin-react needs to move in lockstep.

## Verification
- `bun run build` clean
- `bun run test` green (295 pass currently)
- Manual smoke test of dev server

title: Migrate to ESLint 9/10 + flat config (eslint 8 is EOL)
description:
## Why
ESLint 8 reached end-of-life October 2024 — no more security patches. We're on 8.57.1. Not currently flagged by dependabot, but staying on an unmaintained linter is a slow-burn security/hygiene risk.

## What's needed
- `eslint` 8.57.1 → 10.x (or 9.x if 10 is too fresh)
- `@typescript-eslint/eslint-plugin` 6.x → 8.x (only 8.x supports eslint 9/10)
- `@typescript-eslint/parser` 6.x → 8.x
- `eslint-plugin-react-hooks` 4.x → 7.x
- `eslint-plugin-react-refresh` 0.4.x → 0.5.x
- Migrate `.eslintrc*` (legacy config) → `eslint.config.js` (flat config — required from eslint 9+)

## Risks / coupling
- Flat config is a structural rewrite, not a version bump
- typescript-eslint 8 dropped a few rule names — may surface new lint errors that need fixing in source
- CI workflow (.github/workflows/ci.yml) calls `bun run lint` — should keep working without changes once package.json is updated

## Scope decision
Standalone PR. Independent of the vite bump. Can land before or after.

## Verification
- `bun run lint` clean against the existing source
- `bun run lint:fix` produces no diff (idempotent)
- CI workflow still passes

title: Structured units: type IngredientDto.unit as an enum instead of free-form String
acceptance_criteria: Unit enum covers all units the existing data uses, no Other(String) fallback. FromStr handles aliasing (lb → Pound, tsp → Teaspoon, etc). Unknown units at ingest reject with a clear error listing valid values. One-shot migration walks existing ingredients, attempts FromStr on each, reports any failures so the maintainer can expand the enum before re-running. unit_converter.rs rewritten against the enum. ShoppingService aggregation uses typed equality. MCP schemas + UI + URL-import parser updated. The staples and rounding classifiers migrate from string-matching to category-matching when this lands.

title: Expose shopping amounts + pantry split through MCP + UI (ride fewd-p3j + fewd-wm6)
description:
Ride the service-layer work in the two preceding shopping beads (rounding in fewd-p3j, pantry staple classification in fewd-wm6) to give both the LLM and the human user the better shopping view.

**Response shape** (shared across surfaces):

    {
      "items_to_buy": [ShoppingListItem...],
      "pantry_staples_to_verify": [ShoppingListItem...]
    }

Both arrays use rounded amounts from fewd-p3j.

**MCP**: Add format parameter to get_shopping_list — 'recipe' returns today's flat list with exact math (for cases where the LLM wants precise figures — portion-scaling debugging, etc); 'shopping' (default) returns the two-section shape with rounded amounts. Tool description makes clear the pantry_staples_to_verify array is a prompt for the user to check their pantry, not items to put on the list.

**UI**: Shopping tab renders items_to_buy as the main list. pantry_staples_to_verify rendered as a collapsed section at the bottom, one click to expand — 'Check you have these N pantry staples' or similar. Default collapsed so the primary list is the focus.

The rename of the mode ('Shopping amounts' vs 'Recipe amounts') still applies for the MCP format parameter and any UI toggle. A user who wants the old unified exact-math view flips to Recipe amounts.

Scope stays wiring-only: no new service-layer logic (all in the rounding + staples beads); this bead just plumbs through the two surfaces.
acceptance_criteria: MCP get_shopping_list accepts format: 'recipe' | 'shopping' with shopping as default, returning the two-array shape for shopping format. UI Shopping tab renders items_to_buy prominently with pantry_staples_to_verify in a collapsed section. Both routes call through the same service-layer function from fewd-wm6.
notes: Preserve these existing-behavior invariants when touching ShoppingService output (called out by live Claude Desktop consumer as the tool's strongest properties): (1) per-ingredient source breakdown — each aggregated item carries a list of contributing (meal_id, date, meal_type, recipe_name, amount) so the consumer can answer 'why is there extra flour?'; (2) aggregation shape is total + sources, not total OR sources — keep both; (3) range math preserves Range { min, max } through scaling, doesn't collapse to Single on convenient simplification.

title: Integration test: MCP-created meal round-trips to planner-renderable shape
description:
The 'lowercase dinner' bug (commit db20f56) was a convention-mismatch that no existing test caught because every test treated the two surfaces independently: MCP tests verified the tool stored *something*, UI tests verified rendering from hardcoded fixtures. Nothing checked that the thing MCP writes is the shape the UI reads.

Add an integration test in server/tests/ that:

1. Spins up a test DB via the existing setup_db() pattern.
2. Exercises the MCP create_meal path end-to-end (ideally invoking the FewdMcp handler or, failing that, create_meal_input_to_dto followed by MealService::create).
3. Reads back via MealService::get_all_for_date_range (the same path the /api/meals HTTP endpoint uses, which is what MealPlanner.tsx calls).
4. Asserts the returned meal_type matches whatever canonical form the web UI filters on — the strict-equality values in MealPlanner.tsx ('Breakfast', 'Lunch', 'Dinner', 'Snack').

Same shape for any future convention the UI enforces: if the UI expects a particular case for tags, servings_count rounding, date formatting, etc, the round-trip test catches mismatches before the user hits them.

This is not a 'write more unit tests' exercise — unit tests cover their own sides cleanly. The gap this closes is cross-boundary: MCP writes + HTTP API reads need to agree on conventions the compiler can't enforce (since meal_type is String, tags are JSON-encoded Vec<String>, etc). Separate from the enum work in the companion bead — the enum would make one class of this impossible, but not all of them (tag casing, date normalization, etc stay as String-level conventions).
acceptance_criteria: New integration test file or new test in existing tests/service_tests.rs. Creates a meal via the MCP path, reads it back via the HTTP-equivalent path, asserts meal_type matches the canonical UI expectation. Failing version of this test (without the fix in db20f56) catches the bug.

title: Promote meal_type to a Rust enum across DTO + entity + API
description:
Today Meal.meal_type is free-form TEXT in SQLite and plain String in Rust (CreateMealDto, entities::meal::Model, MealBrief). The web UI enforces Title Case via strict equality in MealPlanner.tsx; nothing else does. Consequence: the MCP tool storing lowercase 'dinner' (commit db20f56) silently broke the planner cell rendering even though the partial-recipe warning rendered fine. A type system would have made this impossible.

Shape: a MealType enum in server/src/dto.rs with Breakfast / Lunch / Dinner / Snack variants, implementing:
- serde Serialize/Deserialize with rename_all='PascalCase' so the wire format matches the UI's 'Dinner'/'Lunch'/etc convention
- FromStr with case-insensitive parsing (accepts 'dinner', 'DINNER', etc — normalizes on decode)
- Display → the canonical Title Case string
- From<MealType> for String for DB storage continuity

Apply it everywhere Meal.meal_type crosses a boundary: the SeaORM Model (as a typed column if feasible, or keep String at DB layer with conversion at service boundaries), all DTOs (CreateMealDto / UpdateMealDto / PersonServingDto), the MCP schemas (CreateMealInput / MealBrief / ServingOut), and the TypeScript types in src/types/meal.ts.

Follow-up concerns to plan for during build: (1) existing DB rows may contain non-canonical strings from before the canonicalization fix — include a migration that normalizes existing meal_type values, (2) the PersonServingDto lives in meal servings JSON so its meal_type won't be affected — but the UPDATE route's DTO ingest needs to validate consistently, (3) canonical_meal_type in server/src/mcp/schemas/meals.rs can delete — the enum's FromStr absorbs it.
acceptance_criteria: MealType enum exists with consistent serialization across DB, HTTP API, MCP, and TypeScript. An attempt to store a non-canonical value via any write path (MCP, HTTP PUT, direct SQL) either normalizes or rejects. Migration normalizes any pre-existing non-canonical rows. No Rust code reads or compares meal_type as a raw string anymore.

title: UI: Recipes tab filter panel + curated view toggle (ride fewd-0kk)
description:
Once fewd-0kk adds the full filter set and a RecipeService::curated() function, the Recipes tab should surface both capabilities:

1. Filter panel: tags multi-select, time slider, rating threshold, favorites-only toggle, 'haven't made in N months' toggle, 'safe for [person chips]' persona-dislikes multi-select. Route handlers pass filter params through to RecipeService.

2. Curated view toggle: a view-mode switch (tabs, segmented control, or similar) so the user can flip between 'all recipes (filterable)' and 'curated shortlist' — the same shortlist list_curated_recipes returns to the LLM. This gives humans the same 'what do I actually cook?' view Claude gets, and — useful for trust/debugging — lets the user see exactly what the LLM sees. Both surfaces call the same RecipeService::curated() so the curation policy is in one place.

Structure:
- Service-layer curation lives in fewd-0kk. This bead is UI + routes only.
- Extend GET /api/recipes with filter query params, and add GET /api/recipes/curated for the shortlist.
- In src/components/, build the filter panel and the view-mode toggle. Filters apply only in 'all' view; curated view has no filters (it's already a policy-driven selection).

UX decisions to make during build (not prescribing here):
- Always-visible vs collapsible filter panel.
- URL encodes filter + view state? (Shareable links are nice.)
- Default view: 'all' preserves existing behavior; 'curated' is a nicer first-impression but a change. Lean 'all' unless we have data to say otherwise.

UI-specific scope note NOT shared with MCP: a human scrolling the Recipes tab legitimately wants to see all 150 recipes in 'all' view. The MCP rule ('no full-archive tool') is about LLM token cost; don't let it bleed into the UI's 'all' view.
acceptance_criteria: Recipes tab has: (1) a view-mode toggle flipping between 'all' and 'curated' (curated returns the same shortlist as list_curated_recipes, via RecipeService::curated); (2) a filter panel exposing at least tags, time budget, favorites-only, and persona-dislikes when 'all' is active. Filter state reflected in URL OR persistent local state (decide during build). Route handlers are thin — all filter and curation logic lives in RecipeService per the project's thin-handlers convention.

title: search_recipes: dietary-goals matching (low-carb, vegetarian, gluten-free, etc.)
description:
fewd-0kk deliberately skipped dietary-goals matching because Person.dietary_goals is free-form text ('low-carb', 'trying to eat more vegetables', 'pescatarian') and Recipe has no structured diet fields — only a free-form tags array. Matching 'is this recipe low-carb?' needs either structured nutrition data or AI judgment, both of which fewd lacks today.

Cheapest useful improvement: enforce a conventional diet-tag vocabulary on recipes (low-carb, vegan, vegetarian, gluten-free, pescatarian, dairy-free, nut-free, keto, ...) and let the LLM translate a person's free-form goals into tag queries. Concretely:

1. Document a recommended diet-tag taxonomy (somewhere discoverable — README? A new 'tag conventions' doc? A dedicated tool that returns the list?).

2. Decide whether to enforce it in create_recipe (strict: reject unknown diet tags) or just suggest it (soft: the LLM populates diet-relevant tags when parsing recipes).

3. Add diet-tag awareness to the MCP so the LLM knows which tags represent diet constraints vs. flavor / cuisine. This probably belongs in the search_recipes tool description or a new tool surface like list_diet_tags.

4. Optionally: extract diet-relevant keywords from Person.dietary_goals at filter time and fuzzy-match against recipe tags, so 'plan dinners for Steve (low-carb goals)' resolves without the LLM having to manually translate 'low-carb goal' → 'tags=[low-carb]'.
acceptance_criteria: At least a documented diet-tag vocabulary. Either LLM-translation path (docs + encouraging the LLM to read dietary_goals and query by tag) or automatic-translation path (server reads dietary_goals and adds tag filters). Sample conversation verified: 'plan dinners that fit everyone's dietary goals' produces a non-trivial narrow without the LLM having to inspect every recipe's ingredient list.

title: search_recipes: favorites-based ranking (prefer_for_persons)
description:
Companion to the dislikes filter (fewd-0kk). Favorites is a ranking concern rather than a filter — 'Alice loves pasta' means pasta recipes should surface higher when Alice is in the group, not that non-pasta recipes should be excluded.

Open design questions to resolve when building:

1. Shape: extend search_recipes with a prefer_for_persons: Vec<String> param that sorts matches by 'favorites hit count,' OR add a separate rank_recipes tool that takes a candidate set and ranks it, OR add a weight to list_curated_recipes that tilts the shortlist toward specific people's favorites.

2. Matching rule: same substring caveat as dislikes — Alice's 'pasta' favorite substring-matches recipe tags / names / ingredients, but 'pasta salad' vs 'pasta' — do we rank both or just exact matches? Document whatever rule we pick.

3. Combining signals: when multiple people are listed, sum hits across them? Max? Intersect (recipes that favor ALL listed people)? Different questions suit different user intents — 'dinner for Alice' vs 'dinner the whole family likes.'

4. Pagination becomes meaningful here — if ranking is in play, 'top 20 by fit' is a useful query shape even when the raw filter returns more.
acceptance_criteria: Decide the three design questions above with documented rationale. Favorites ranking queryable via at least one of the shapes above. Ranking criteria explicit in tool description so the LLM can reason about what 'prefer' means.

title: Cooking mode: multi-recipe 'cooking session' (select up to 3 recipes from search)
description:
Extends cooking mode to support cooking 2-3 recipes in parallel — common case when preparing a meal with a main + sides.

Pattern: compare-cart workflow on the Recipes list.
- On RecipeManager (recipes list/search), each recipe card gets a checkbox/'Add to cooking session' affordance
- A small floating bar appears once 1+ recipe is selected, showing the selection (with dismiss-each), with a 'Start cooking session' button
- Max selection: ~3 recipes (UI gracefully blocks 4th selection with a hint)
- Clicking 'Start cooking session' navigates to a multi-recipe cooking view — likely /cook?ids=<slug1>,<slug2> or similar
- Multi-recipe layout: 2 recipes = side-by-side columns. 3 recipes = either tabbed or 3-col on wide viewports / stacked-with-sticky-tabs on narrow

Reuses:
- The 'chrome hidden' context from fewd-pqb
- The CookingView component (likely refactored to render a single recipe; a new MultiCookingView composes N of them)

Open questions for brainstorm before implementation:
- 3 recipes side-by-side may not fit on narrow screens — tabs vs scrolling vs stacking?
- Do ingredients merge into a unified shopping-style list, or stay per-recipe?
- Wake lock single shared lifecycle (already a singleton API)
- Selection state: live in a hook, persist across navigation? lost on reload?

Depends on: fewd-pqb (calm focused view), and ideally the ADHD-enhancements follow-up so the multi-recipe view inherits step check-off etc.

Originated from user spitballing during fewd-pqb brainstorm: 'I could see how I might be wanting to cook two or even three recipes at once... add a couple/few to a list, then click a button to go into compare/cooking mode.'

title: Add cooking mode — distraction-free recipe view
description:
When a user is actively cooking a recipe, they want to focus on just that recipe. The current detail page is a decent start but still surrounds the content with app chrome (top navigation, back links, edit/adapt/scale buttons). In a real kitchen, those controls are noise — and phones dim the screen. A dedicated cooking mode should present the recipe big, calm, and stable.

Core idea: from the recipe detail page, a 'Cook this' (or similar) button enters a focused view that shows only the recipe's ingredients and instructions, at larger-than-default sizing, with the app's normal nav hidden. An obvious 'Exit' affordance (plus Escape key) returns to the normal detail view.

Nice-to-have extensions (could be follow-ups, not required for the first cut):
- Use the Wake Lock API to keep the screen on while the mode is active.
- Check-off state for ingredients (crossed off as used) — local state, not persisted.
- Bigger step-numbered instructions, optionally 'focus current step' with next/prev controls.
- Timer integration for prep/cook/total times.

Scope this issue to just the focused-view + exit + wake lock. Leave the ingredient checklist, step focus, and timers as follow-ups once the base flow feels right.
design:
## Routing vs. local state

Prefer encoding the mode in the URL (query param `?mode=cook` on the existing /recipes/:slug route) rather than a separate /cook/:slug route. Reason: keeps the resource identity in one place, and reload/share naturally preserves the mode.

## Rendering

RecipeDetailPage branches on the mode param:
- default: existing RecipeDetail component (all affordances)
- cook: a new CookingView component that renders only name + ingredients + instructions, full-width, larger type, no chrome.

The app shell (App.tsx) needs a way to hide the top/sub nav for this route. Simplest: a context flag 'chrome: hidden' set by the cook-mode page via useEffect, consumed by the layout. Alternative: a separate route layout that opts out of the shell. Either is fine — context is lighter-touch.

## Wake Lock

`navigator.wakeLock.request('screen')` — acquire on enter, release on exit and on visibilitychange=hidden. Guard behind a feature check; no-op on Safari desktop and older browsers. Do not surface errors to the user — just log and continue.

## Testing

- Component test: entering /recipes/:slug?mode=cook renders ingredients + instructions and hides nav.
- Component test: Escape key exits to ?mode absent.
- Hook test for wake lock can be skipped — navigator.wakeLock is a global mock.
acceptance_criteria:
- A clearly-labeled button on the recipe detail page enters cooking mode.
- In cooking mode: top nav and sub-nav are hidden; recipe name, ingredients, and instructions remain visible at larger-than-default sizing.
- Edit / adapt / scale / favorite controls are hidden in cooking mode (kept out of the way — still reachable by exiting).
- An 'Exit' button and the Escape key both return to the normal detail view.
- Screen stays awake while cooking mode is active (Wake Lock API where supported; gracefully no-op where not).
- URL reflects the mode (e.g. /recipes/<slug>?mode=cook) so reload and shared links stay in cooking mode.
- Works on narrow (mobile) and wide (desktop) viewports.
notes: Originated from a user moment while cooking: 'I want to view it and only it, not all of the other recipes.' The existing RecipeDetailPage already isolates to one recipe; what's missing is the *reduction* to just the content a cook needs at arm's length. Treat this as a presentation layer on top of the existing detail page, not a new data model.
close_reason: Closed

title: Use slugs instead of UUIDs in recipe URLs (pinned at creation)
description:
Today recipe detail URLs embed UUIDs (e.g. /recipes/abc-123-def-456 and /cocktails/recipes/abc-123-def-456). They work for sharing, but they're opaque and ugly. Switch the URL-visible identifier to a human-readable slug derived from the recipe name (e.g. /recipes/pizza-margherita, /cocktails/recipes/negroni).

Depends on: fewd-84t (routing introduced there; this extends it).
design:
## Decision: slugs pinned at creation

Slugs are generated once when the recipe is created and never updated when the recipe's name changes. This keeps shared links stable at the cost of mild staleness (URL can diverge from the displayed name). Rejected alternative: maintaining a `recipe_slugs` history table for redirects on rename — doubles the complexity and isn't needed at this project's scale.

## Backend changes

- New migration: add `slug TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE` to `recipes` and `drink_recipes`. Backfill from existing `name` with collision suffixes (`-2`, `-3`, …). Create a unique index.
- Add `server/src/services/slug.rs` (or a module-level helper) with a `slugify(name) -> String` function: lowercase, strip/normalize accents, replace whitespace/punctuation with single hyphens, trim, cap length (~80 chars). Can use a lightweight dep (`slug` crate) or hand-roll.
- `RecipeService::create` / `DrinkRecipeService::create`: compute base slug, then loop `base`, `base-2`, `base-3`, … until unique (service-side check against DB, not relying on insert-retry — cleaner).
- Route dispatch: `GET /recipes/:id_or_slug` checks whether the param matches the UUID v4 regex; if yes → lookup by id, else → lookup by slug. Same for `/drink-recipes/:id_or_slug`. All other routes (update, delete, favorite, scale, enhance, adapt) continue to use UUID so mutations have a stable key.

## Frontend changes

- `src/types/recipe.ts` / `src/types/drinkRecipe.ts`: add `slug: string`.
- `src/hooks/useRecipes.ts` / `useDrinkRecipes.ts`: `useRecipe(idOrSlug)` works unchanged because the backend now resolves either. (No dedicated `useRecipeBySlug` needed.)
- Route path stays `/recipes/:id` and `/cocktails/recipes/:id` — rename the param to `:slug` for clarity if desired, but not required.
- Update the three `navigate('/recipes/${recipe.id}')` call sites and the drink recipe card `<Link to=...>` to use `recipe.slug`.
- After create/import/adapt, navigate to the new recipe's slug.

## Why pinned at creation

- Shared links never rot.
- No redirect table, no 301/302 flow, no stale-cache concerns.
- Minor UX cost: if a recipe is renamed meaningfully (e.g. "Untitled Recipe" → "Grandma's Bolognese"), the URL still says `untitled-recipe`. Acceptable — the page renders the current name.
acceptance_criteria:
- New migration runs clean on both fresh and populated databases. All existing recipes and drink recipes get a non-null, unique slug after migration.
- Creating a new recipe assigns a slug derived from the name. Creating a second recipe with the same name gets a `-2` suffix (and `-3`, etc.).
- Opening /recipes/<slug> and /cocktails/recipes/<slug> loads the correct detail page. Refresh works (SPA fallback already in place).
- Opening /recipes/<uuid> still works for backwards compatibility with any outstanding links.
- Renaming a recipe does NOT change its slug — the URL stays stable.
- All list-page card clicks navigate using slugs.
- Frontend build, lint, tests, dprint, and Rust clippy/tests pass.
- Smoke test: open a recipe by slug in a fresh tab; rename it; verify the URL still resolves and shows the new name.
notes: Follow-up to fewd-4q0 test work (2026-04-18): when slugs land, add an integration test case in src/routes/RecipeDetailPage.test.tsx that renders the page via a slug-based initialPath (e.g. '/recipes/pasta') and verifies the detail page resolves via GET /api/recipes/pasta. AC currently says slug-based URL test lives there.
close_reason: Implemented slug-in-URL feature: migration adds unique slug column with backfill + collision suffixing; slugify helper (6 unit tests) handles accents/punctuation/empty-fallback; backend resolves UUID or slug on GET routes; frontend navigates by slug in all 10 call sites; added 4 new backend integration tests and 1 new frontend integration test covering slug-based detail resolution.

title: Build an MCP server for fewd
description: Expose fewd's domain (family members, meals, recipes, cocktails, shopping lists, bar inventory) via a Model Context Protocol server so AI assistants (Claude Code, Claude Desktop, etc.) can query and mutate data directly. This enables use cases like: 'plan this week's meals around what's in the pantry', 'suggest a cocktail with what's in my bar', 'add a recipe from this URL'. Open questions to resolve during design: (1) language/runtime — Rust (reuse server crate as library) vs. TypeScript (thin HTTP wrapper over existing Axum API); (2) which operations to expose as tools vs. resources; (3) auth model for multi-user scenarios; (4) transport (stdio for local, HTTP/SSE for remote).
close_reason: Delivered via PR #10 (commit 758e851), merged into main 2026-04-25. The MCP server is live: 10 tools + 1 resource at /mcp on the existing Axum router, bearer-name auth, Streamable HTTP transport, real end-to-end usage with Claude Desktop already validated by the project owner. Out-of-scope work (recipe discovery redesign, shopping-list ergonomics, IngredientDto.unit enum, name/prep split, MealType enum, round-trip integration test, UI filter panel, historical meals, grocery sections) is filed as beads chained off this one — those are now unblocked.

title: Bump TypeScript 5 → 6 (when stable)
description:
## Why
TypeScript 6 is on the horizon (current outdated check shows 6.0.3 available). Mostly an evolution from 5.9, but a major bump usually unlocks rule deprecations and 1-2 minor surface-level breaks.

## What's needed
- `typescript` 5.9.3 → 6.x

Defer until ecosystem catches up (vite plugin-react, vitest, eslint-typescript all need to support TS 6). Recheck after fewd-5tc and fewd-zmv land. P4 — backlog.

- `bun run build` clean (`tsc && vite build`)
- `bun run test` green
- `bun run lint` clean

title: Shopping list: classify each ingredient by grocery-store section (produce, meat, pantry, etc.)
description:
Shopping list items today surface in alphabetical order. Real-world shopping flows section-by-section: do produce, then meat, then pantry, then dairy. Most smart grocery apps (Instacart, AnyList, etc.) group by section as their default view.

**Target shape**: add a section field to each ShoppingListItem (and to the rounded GroceryListItem from fewd-p3j). Render as a field, not as a grouping — consumers decide how to present.

    pub enum GrocerySection {
        Produce,       // fresh fruits, vegetables, herbs
        Meat,          // meat, poultry, fish, seafood
        Dairy,         // milk, cheese, yogurt, butter, eggs
        Pantry,        // grains, oils, vinegars, canned goods, flours, sugars
        Frozen,        // anything with 'frozen' in the name, ice cream, etc.
        Bakery,        // breads, pastries (human-baked, not home-baked)
        Beverages,     // drinks, wines, beers, sodas
        Other,         // anything that doesn't fit — stays visible but de-emphasized
    }

Response shape:

    {
      "items_to_buy": [
        { "ingredient_name": "carrots", "section": "produce", ... },
        { "ingredient_name": "chicken", "section": "meat", ... },
        ...
      ],
      "pantry_staples_to_verify": [...]  // staples already have implicit section = pantry
    }

**Classification approach**: hand-curated dictionary mapping ingredient names to sections. ~200-400 common ingredients covers the practical cases. Substring-matching with care ('chicken broth' → pantry, 'chicken breast' → meat). Unknown → Other, surfaced so the maintainer can extend the dictionary deliberately.

**Why dictionary over alternatives**:
- Stored per-ingredient metadata: requires every recipe author to classify. Burden for marginal gain. (Relates to 'canonical Ingredient table' refactor which is its own much-bigger bead — not in this scope.)
- LLM call per ingredient: expensive, latency on every shopping-list request, deterministic dictionary is better.

**MCP/UI**:
- MCP: section field on every ShoppingListItem. Tool description notes the LLM can group narratively ('here's produce: ...; here's meat: ...') or just render flat.
- UI: Shopping tab renders items grouped by section (collapsible sections, produce first by default). User can toggle to flat/alphabetical.

**Not in scope** (deliberately):
- A canonical Ingredient table / ingredient-by-id refactor. That's a much bigger structural change, a separate bead if it ever matters.
- Store-specific aisle layouts. Sections are generic; real stores vary.
- Automatic re-classification when ingredient names change. Dictionary is source of truth; updates happen when the maintainer edits it.

Depends conceptually on fewd-p3j (rounding) + fewd-wm6 (staples) since all three ride the same GroceryListItem shape; actual dependency is just on the shape existing.
acceptance_criteria: GrocerySection enum covers the common cases with Other fallback. Classifier is a hand-curated name→section dictionary of at least 200 common ingredients, with documented substring-match rules for ambiguous names ('chicken broth' vs 'chicken breast'). Unknown ingredients classify as Other and surface for dictionary expansion. MCP ShoppingListItem carries the section field. UI Shopping tab renders grouped-by-section by default with a toggle for flat view.
notes: Preserve these existing-behavior invariants when touching ShoppingService output (called out by live Claude Desktop consumer as the tool's strongest properties): (1) per-ingredient source breakdown — each aggregated item carries a list of contributing (meal_id, date, meal_type, recipe_name, amount) so the consumer can answer 'why is there extra flour?'; (2) aggregation shape is total + sources, not total OR sources — keep both; (3) range math preserves Range { min, max } through scaling, doesn't collapse to Single on convenient simplification.

title: list_meals: expose historical meals for pattern matching in LLM planning
description: Today list_meals and get_shopping_list take arbitrary start/end dates, but the MCP use case so far is forward-looking: 'plan this week,' 'what's this Sunday's dinner.' Worth adding surface area or convention so the LLM can also reach backward for *pattern* data — e.g. 'we've had pasta three Sundays running, suggest something else,' 'Amanda hasn't been on the meal plan in 10 days,' 'what did we do last Thanksgiving week.' Not a v1-scope concern — shelve until the baseline MCP use pattern has some data behind it. Then decide whether a new tool (e.g. meal_history) is cleaner than just letting list_meals reach further back, and whether any aggregates (most-frequent recipes in window, cuisine distribution) should be surfaced directly rather than making the LLM do its own reduction on raw lists.
acceptance_criteria: Decide whether historical patterns live inside list_meals (no API change, LLM is just trusted to query back further) or in a dedicated meal_history / meal_patterns tool with pre-aggregated summaries. Surface whatever fewd-egl-style user scenario motivates the choice.

value: rmcp 1.5 Streamable HTTP transport wraps the ENTIRE http::request::Parts as a single value inside RequestContext::extensions — it does NOT flatten Axum-layer extensions into context.extensions. To read state added by an Axum middleware from inside a tool, do TWO lookups: context.extensions.get::<axum::http::request::Parts>() → parts.extensions.get::<YourType>(). The helper fewd_lib::mcp::handler::authenticated_name shows the pattern. Also: rmcp::handler::server::tool::Parameters is private; use rmcp::handler::server::wrapper::Parameters instead (rmcp docs show the private path in some examples).

User prefers PR review for production-touching changes (migrations, deploys, schema work) before merge to main, even on private personal repos. This emerged after a slug-migration deploy went sideways on dietpi (fewd-nwi) and the user wanted a review pass on what had already been merged. For future sessions: when implementing migrations, dep updates with backend impact, or anything that touches deploy pipelines, default to working on a feature branch and opening a PR rather than committing direct to main, even when in auto mode.

When working on fewd's MCP server (fewd-egl or follow-ups), reference ~/Development/projects/selfie/crates/mcp-server/ as the prior-art pattern. Selfie uses rmcp 1.2 (fewd should use 1.5) with the #[tool_router] / #[tool] / #[tool_handler] macro style, Arc<Service> fields on the handler struct (no Mutex), rmcp::ErrorData (aliased McpError) with McpError::invalid_params for validation, and a tool_result() helper at server.rs:828-834 that wraps serde_json::to_string_pretty in CallToolResult::success/error. Selfie is stdio-only, so HTTP transport wiring is net-new for fewd.

Vitest 1.6.x in this repo has flaky module-cache failures on consecutive runs: 'React.createContext is not a function' or cookie 'parse' export errors hit a different test file each invocation. Caused by ESM/CJS interop with react-router 7 + @tanstack/react-query + cookie@1.x. Workaround: rm -rf node_modules/.vite before running tests. Real fix tracked in fewd-5tc (vitest 1 → 3 bump). When pre-push CI hook (.claude/hooks/ci-before-push.sh) blocks on this for non-source-changing pushes (tag deletes, release publishes), SKIP_CI_HOOK=1 is the documented one-off bypass — but only after manually verifying tests pass with a clean cache.

Design principles validated by live Claude Desktop feedback on fewd's MCP surface (PR #10). Apply these when extending the MCP (fewd-0kk filter work, fewd-2mk ranking, fewd-08x diet matching, and any future tools):

1. Error on unknown references with actionable messages. When a tool input names a slug/person/tag that doesn't exist, return an invalid_params (-32602) MCP error whose text points at the discovery tool ('call list_people to see valid names'). Turns silent corruption into a retry loop the LLM can recover from without human intervention. Already implemented for create_meal's person_name / recipe_slug resolution; generalize to every new write tool.

2. Cross-reference tools in their descriptions. 'Use list_recipes to find slugs,' 'prefer search_recipes to avoid duplicates' — these teach the LLM the API's internal graph without a separate docs page, and they do real work at tool-selection time. Every new tool description should name the upstream tool the LLM should usually call first.

3. Dual-expose important context as both tool AND resource when it matters. Resources are for user-side attachment (paperclip UI); tools are for LLM autonomous fetch. The family overview needs both. When adding future context-heavy surface (e.g. nutrition summary, recent-meals digest), default to dual exposure unless there's a reason not to.

4. Name discoverability > namespace tidiness. get_family_overview beats family_overview or render_family because the LLM's tool-selection is substring-matchy on name+description. Prefer verbs that match user intent ('get', 'find', 'list') over domain-jargon names.

5. Do the 'right thing by default' server-side. Slug generation from names, case-insensitive name lookups, empty-field sentinels in output (the _none_ rendering), default meal_type normalization — don't push these to the LLM when the server can do them deterministically.

6. Iterate output format based on live session feedback. The _none recorded_ → _none_ shift and the always-present Notes line both came from real Claude Desktop sessions. This is the *primary* signal source for MCP ergonomics — not speculation, not theoretical API review. Build the habit of asking 'how did that feel?' after real use.

7. Respect the domain model's expressiveness at the MCP boundary — don't flatten it. Fractional servings_count: f64 (Cleo at 0.5), tagged amount enums (Single | Range), structured ingredient amounts with unit — all survive intact through the tool surface. Collapsing these to 'simpler' shapes (int servings, free-text amounts) in the name of LLM-friendliness quietly breaks the math downstream. If the LLM struggles with rich structure, fix it via tool description + examples, not via type downgrades.

fewd is in pre-scaled-usage state: prod DB (home server) has ~20-30 recipes, 4 family members, handful of meals planned; daily active use by family is minimal. User explicit stance (2026-04-24): data migration cost should not be a constraint on design decisions. Even a complete prod-DB drop would be survivable and not catastrophic. Apply this to bead scoping and design choices:

1. Don't soften type-system strictness to preserve existing-row deserialization. Prefer stricter types (e.g. closed enums, no Other(String) escape hatches for data shapes) and migrate.

2. Don't defer cleanups 'because existing data' — a best-effort auto-split, a human-reviewed normalization, or even a from-scratch re-entry of the handful of recipes is all acceptable cost.

3. When scoping refactors, price in the write-once migration as a small cost rather than an ongoing tax. Correctness concerns about the migration ITSELF (ambiguous parses, fuzzy splits) still matter — but the raw cost of running SQL / walking data / re-entering is not a reason to hesitate.

4. This explicitly does NOT mean 'move fast break things for remote users' — it means that the small current user base and low data volume gives design space that larger apps wouldn't have. Use it deliberately, not sloppily.

Reconsider this posture if/when family use grows (~100+ recipes, consistent daily meal planning, multi-user production load). Until then, bias toward clean long-term design over migration-friendly design.

User's other web projects are all Rust + Svelte. fewd is the React outlier — the Svelte 5 migration question is driven by cross-project consistency, not React frustration. If a future Svelte/SvelteKit question comes up for this repo, weight that motivation appropriately. Estimate landed at 2–4 weeks; not currently scheduled, no beads issue filed (left as exploration per user direction 2026-04-24).

Migration anti-pattern in this repo: do NOT edit an already-applied migration source in-place to add columns/changes. SeaORM tracks migrations by name, so any DB that ran the original version will skip the modified version and silently drift from the schema the code expects. This bit prod once via the slug column (fewd-nwi, fixed by m20260424_000012). Rule: every schema change must be a NEW migration file. Even during pre-release, treat the dietpi deploy as production-equivalent.

The primary user of fewd's cook mode benefits significantly from ADHD-friendly UI patterns. Cook-mode and related UI should prioritize visual anchoring (clear 'where am I now?' affordances), reduced cognitive load (no decisions about where to look), and resilience to interruption (state survives walking away). This shapes priority of features like step check-off, current-step highlight, and persisted progress. Originated from fewd-pqb brainstorm 2026-04-19.

When SSH or just deploy commands to dietpi.local hang/timeout intermittently from this user's machine, suspect their router's intrusion-detection blocking cross-VLAN traffic before suspecting dietpi itself, the deploy script, or the network. Confirmed by user 2026-04-25 after a hang during fewd-nwi rollout. Workaround: wait it out or have user adjust router rules. Don't waste time tailing journalctl on dietpi or rebuilding the binary in this scenario — the SSH connection itself is being dropped at the network layer.
